title: Earth Observation Satellites
subtitle: How orbital sentinels monitor our planet: from crops and floods to melting glaciers and military intelligence.

hero.prompt: Photorealistic illustration for a science encyclopedia: a polar-orbiting Earth observation satellite above the sunlit terminator line of Earth. Satellite features large solar panels, a suite of optical and radar sensor pods. Below: vivid Earth with visible cloud swirls, ocean, forest, agricultural patchwork, and river deltas. Satellite scans a swath of land shown as a glowing grid overlay. Hard sci-fi style, dark space background, technically detailed. Add the following text labels on the image: "sun-synchronous orbit", "multispectral sensor", "radar antenna", "ground swath".
hero.alt: An Earth observation satellite in sun-synchronous orbit above a sunlit Earth showing agricultural fields and cloud patterns
hero.caption: A modern Earth observation satellite typically carries several sensor types simultaneously. Optical cameras capture reflected sunlight, radar antennas image through cloud cover, and thermal detectors sense the surface's own heat emission.

paragraphs: Imagine trying to understand the health of a patient without touching them. You observe skin color, temperature, breathing — and draw conclusions. Earth observation from orbit works exactly like that: the planet is the patient, satellites are the physicians watching from above, reading its condition without ever landing on its surface.

The underlying principle is straightforward. The planet's surface absorbs sunlight, reflects part of it back, and emits its own thermal radiation. Every type of surface — water, sand, vegetation, snow, concrete — does this in its own way, across its own characteristic range of wavelengths. A satellite sensor captures that spectral signature and transmits it to the ground. Algorithms then decode the scene: where is forest, where is wheat, where is a wildfire, where is a flood.

The first steps in this direction came in the mid-twentieth century, with photographs from early weather satellites. But the genuine revolution began in 1972, when the United States launched the first satellite in the Landsat program. It started a continuous archive of Earth imagery that has not stopped since — over fifty years of unbroken observations from orbit. No ground-based monitoring network comes close to matching that.

image.prompt: Photorealistic illustration for a science encyclopedia: side-by-side false-color satellite images comparing the same Amazon river delta location in two different years. Left image shows dense green rainforest coverage. Right image shows significant brown deforestation patches and cleared land. Each image labeled with a year marker. Hard sci-fi style visualization, dark panel background, monospace labels. Add the following text labels on the image: "intact forest", "deforested area", "river", "multi-decade monitoring".
image.alt: Side-by-side satellite comparison of the same Amazon location decades apart, showing extensive deforestation
image.caption: The Landsat archive allows change detection down to individual fields and forest blocks. Amazon deforestation is one of the most intensively studied processes in the entire field of Earth observation.

body.heading: The orbit that always looks at the same time of day
paragraphs: Most Earth observation satellites fly what is called a _sun-synchronous orbit_. This is an orbit at roughly seven hundred kilometers altitude, inclined to the equator by approximately ninety-nine degrees. Due to a subtle interaction between the tilted orbital plane and the slightly flattened shape of the Earth, the orbital plane slowly precesses at exactly the rate needed to keep pace with the Earth's movement around the Sun. The result: the satellite always crosses any given point on the surface at the same local time.

Why does that matter? Comparative image analysis only works when illumination conditions are consistent. A photograph taken at noon and one taken at sunrise have such different shadows, hues, and reflection angles that direct comparison is nearly impossible. Sun-synchronous orbit solves this permanently: images from different years can be placed side by side and differences attributed to real surface change, not changing light. That is why we can confidently state that a glacier has retreated three kilometers over twenty years — not just that it "seems to have shrunk".

A single satellite on such an orbit typically revisits the same ground strip every five to sixteen days, depending on its swath width. But an entire constellation of satellites — the several dozen spacecraft of Planet Labs — has made daily global land coverage at three-meter resolution a reality, available continuously since approximately 2017. That fundamentally changed what planetary monitoring means.

diagram.title: Sun-synchronous orbit geometry
diagram.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 680 340" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <rect width="680" height="340" fill="rgba(10,15,25,0.5)"/>

  <!-- Title -->
  <text x="340" y="22" fill="#aabbcc" font-family="monospace" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">Sun-Synchronous Orbit — Geometry</text>

  <!-- Earth -->
  <circle cx="340" cy="180" r="80" fill="#1a2a3a" stroke="#334455" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="340" y="185" fill="#aabbcc" font-family="monospace" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">Earth</text>

  <!-- Equator line -->
  <line x1="260" y1="180" x2="420" y2="180" stroke="#334455" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,4"/>
  <text x="428" y="184" fill="#667788" font-family="monospace" font-size="9">equator</text>

  <!-- Sun rays from left -->
  <line x1="30" y1="120" x2="120" y2="160" stroke="#ff8844" stroke-width="1.5" opacity="0.7"/>
  <line x1="30" y1="180" x2="105" y2="180" stroke="#ff8844" stroke-width="1.5" opacity="0.7"/>
  <line x1="30" y1="240" x2="120" y2="200" stroke="#ff8844" stroke-width="1.5" opacity="0.7"/>
  <text x="20" y="178" fill="#ff8844" font-family="monospace" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle">Sun</text>

  <!-- SSO orbit ellipse (tilted ~99 deg) -->
  <ellipse cx="340" cy="180" rx="145" ry="145" fill="none" stroke="#7bb8ff" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="6,4" opacity="0.7"
    transform="rotate(9,340,180)"/>

  <!-- Satellite dot on orbit -->
  <circle cx="340" cy="35" r="6" fill="#44ff88"/>
  <text x="353" y="32" fill="#44ff88" font-family="monospace" font-size="9">satellite</text>

  <!-- Altitude label -->
  <line x1="340" y1="100" x2="340" y2="35" stroke="#8899aa" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="2,3"/>
  <text x="345" y="72" fill="#8899aa" font-family="monospace" font-size="9">~700 km</text>

  <!-- Ground track arrows -->
  <line x1="260" y1="260" x2="420" y2="260" stroke="#334455" stroke-width="0.5"/>
  <text x="340" y="280" fill="#667788" font-family="monospace" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">ground track (always same local time)</text>

  <!-- 99 deg inclination label -->
  <text x="460" y="100" fill="#7bb8ff" font-family="monospace" font-size="9">inclination ~99°</text>
  <text x="460" y="112" fill="#7bb8ff" font-family="monospace" font-size="9">(near-polar)</text>

  <!-- Revisit -->
  <text x="340" y="315" fill="#8899aa" font-family="monospace" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">Revisit: 5–16 days (single sat) / daily (constellation)</text>
</svg>
diagram.caption: An inclination of approximately ninety-nine degrees makes the orbit nearly polar — the satellite passes over every latitude on Earth. The precession of the orbital plane caused by Earth's equatorial bulge keeps it synchronized with the Sun throughout the year.

body.heading: Optical sensors: visible light and near-infrared
paragraphs: The oldest and most intuitive sensor type is an optical camera that records reflected sunlight in the visible and near-infrared spectrum. The human eye sees wavelengths from roughly 400 to 700 nanometers, but satellite sensors extend into the near-infrared — from 700 to 2500 nanometers — where vegetation, water, and minerals behave very differently from how they appear in visible light.

From these spectral measurements comes one of the most widely used tools in Earth observation: the _normalized difference vegetation index_. The logic is simple: a healthy plant absorbs red light for photosynthesis and simultaneously reflects near-infrared strongly. Dry or stressed vegetation shows the opposite balance. By capturing the contrast between those two channels, agronomists were already estimating regional crop yields from orbit in the 1980s — and today the index underpins precision agriculture operations worldwide.

Spatial resolution is the defining tradeoff: smaller pixel footprint on the ground means finer detail but narrower swath per pass. The European Union's Sentinel-2 satellites image at ten meters per pixel with a five-day revisit cycle and distribute the data free of charge. Commercial WorldView satellites resolve features down to thirty centimeters — at that scale individual vehicles in a parking lot are visible. Between these extremes, the Planet Labs megaconstellation closes the daily-coverage gap at three-meter resolution for a subscription fee.

image.prompt: Photorealistic illustration for a science encyclopedia: false-color satellite image of agricultural fields showing vegetation index mapping. Fields displayed in spectrum from deep red (bare soil, low vegetation) through yellow to bright green (healthy dense crop). Visible field boundaries, irrigation canals, and scattered farm structures. Hard sci-fi data visualization style, dark border frame, monospace labels. Add the following text labels on the image: "healthy crop (high index)", "stressed crop", "bare soil", "irrigation channel".
image.alt: False-color satellite image of farmland with vegetation index map showing healthy crops, stressed zones, and bare soil
image.caption: The normalized difference vegetation index lets farmers detect crop stress zones weeks before the problem becomes visible to the naked eye. In large-scale operations this directly reduces fertilizer and water consumption without sacrificing yield.

body.heading: Synthetic aperture radar: eyes through the clouds
paragraphs: Optical sensors are blind in cloudy weather — and the tropics, where the most dramatic environmental changes occur, are cloud-covered for much of the year. The solution is _synthetic aperture radar_. This is an active sensor: it transmits its own microwave radio signal, records the echo returned from the surface, and constructs an image from that data. Clouds, fog, rain, and darkness are all transparent to microwaves — such a satellite sees equally well day and night, in any weather.

The name "synthetic aperture" reflects the core ingenuity. To produce a sharp image, a real radar antenna would need to be several kilometers wide. Instead, a small physical antenna moves along the orbit, and a computer stitches together hundreds of sequential measurements as if the antenna truly were enormous. The result: centimeter-level sensitivity to surface deformation. Such a radar can detect a city block sinking a few millimeters per year, or a volcanic dome swelling before an eruption.

The European Union's Sentinel-1 satellites form a free open archive of radar imagery dating back to 2014. Their data underpins flood mapping, ground subsidence monitoring, crop monitoring, and ship navigation through sea ice. Commercial operator ICEYE went further: its small satellites revisit any point on Earth multiple times per day — critical for operational disaster response.

diagram.title: Synthetic aperture radar versus optical sensor
diagram.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 680 300" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <rect width="680" height="300" fill="rgba(10,15,25,0.5)"/>

  <!-- Title -->
  <text x="340" y="22" fill="#aabbcc" font-family="monospace" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">Synthetic Aperture Radar vs. Optical Sensor</text>

  <!-- Divider -->
  <line x1="340" y1="35" x2="340" y2="285" stroke="#334455" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,4"/>

  <!-- LEFT: Optical sensor -->
  <text x="170" y="50" fill="#7bb8ff" font-family="monospace" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">Optical Sensor</text>

  <!-- Sun -->
  <circle cx="60" cy="90" r="16" fill="#ff8844" opacity="0.8"/>
  <text x="60" y="120" fill="#ff8844" font-family="monospace" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">Sun</text>

  <!-- Satellite optical -->
  <rect x="140" y="70" width="40" height="20" fill="#334455" stroke="#7bb8ff" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="160" y="83" fill="#7bb8ff" font-family="monospace" font-size="8" text-anchor="middle">sensor</text>

  <!-- Cloud blocking -->
  <ellipse cx="160" cy="155" rx="55" ry="22" fill="#445566" opacity="0.9"/>
  <text x="160" y="158" fill="#aabbcc" font-family="monospace" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">clouds</text>

  <!-- Blocked signal -->
  <line x1="160" y1="91" x2="160" y2="133" stroke="#7bb8ff" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="3,3" opacity="0.5"/>
  <text x="170" y="205" fill="#cc4444" font-family="monospace" font-size="9">BLOCKED</text>
  <text x="160" y="220" fill="#8899aa" font-family="monospace" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">cannot image through clouds</text>

  <!-- RIGHT: SAR -->
  <text x="510" y="50" fill="#44ff88" font-family="monospace" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">Synthetic Aperture Radar</text>

  <!-- SAR satellite -->
  <rect x="480" y="70" width="50" height="20" fill="#334455" stroke="#44ff88" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="505" y="83" fill="#44ff88" font-family="monospace" font-size="8" text-anchor="middle">radar</text>

  <!-- SAR signal goes through cloud -->
  <ellipse cx="505" cy="155" rx="55" ry="22" fill="#445566" opacity="0.9"/>
  <text x="505" y="158" fill="#aabbcc" font-family="monospace" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">clouds</text>

  <!-- Active signal down and echo back -->
  <line x1="505" y1="91" x2="505" y2="133" stroke="#44ff88" stroke-width="1.5" opacity="0.8"/>
  <line x1="505" y1="178" x2="505" y2="240" stroke="#44ff88" stroke-width="1.5" opacity="0.8"/>
  <line x1="490" y1="240" x2="520" y2="240" stroke="#44ff88" stroke-width="1.5" opacity="0.8"/>

  <!-- Ground echo back -->
  <line x1="505" y1="240" x2="505" y2="178" stroke="#ff8844" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4,3" opacity="0.7"/>
  <text x="528" y="244" fill="#ff8844" font-family="monospace" font-size="8">echo</text>

  <text x="505" y="265" fill="#44ff88" font-family="monospace" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">works day/night/through cloud</text>

  <!-- Bottom labels -->
  <text x="160" y="285" fill="#8899aa" font-family="monospace" font-size="8" text-anchor="middle">passive | depends on illumination</text>
  <text x="510" y="285" fill="#8899aa" font-family="monospace" font-size="8" text-anchor="middle">active | weather-independent</text>
</svg>
diagram.caption: An optical sensor is passive: it records reflected sunlight. Clouds or darkness render it useless. Synthetic aperture radar is active: it transmits its own signal and listens for the return echo. Microwaves pass through clouds and precipitation without meaningful attenuation.

body.heading: Thermal infrared and hyperspectral sensors
paragraphs: Thermal infrared radiation is the surface's own heat — not reflected sunlight. Any object above absolute zero emits in this band. The most recent Landsat satellite — the ninth — and the Ecosystem Spaceborne Thermal Radiometer mounted on the International Space Station both map surface temperature at resolutions between sixty and one hundred meters. This enables tracking of abnormal urban heating — the so-called urban heat island effect — finding underground heat leaks, detecting wildfires through dense smoke, and measuring heat stress in agricultural crops.

_Hyperspectral_ sensors represent the next level of detail. Rather than a handful of broad spectral channels, they capture hundreds of narrow, contiguous bands simultaneously, building a complete spectral fingerprint for every pixel. Different minerals, rock types, soil varieties, and plant species all have unique spectral profiles — a hyperspectral image lets scientists read the chemical composition of the surface without a single laboratory sample. Germany's Environmental Mapping and Analysis Program satellite and the Earth Surface Mineral Dust Source Investigation instrument on the space station opened a new chapter: they identify mineral deposits, assess soil degradation, and pinpoint point sources of greenhouse gas emissions from orbit.

image.prompt: Photorealistic illustration for a science encyclopedia: thermal infrared satellite image of a large city at night. Buildings, roads, and industrial areas glow bright orange-red from heat. Parks and water bodies appear cool dark blue-purple. Clear temperature gradient from downtown core to suburban periphery. Hard sci-fi data visualization style, false-color thermal palette, dark background. Add the following text labels on the image: "urban heat island", "cool parkland", "industrial zone", "thermal infrared".
image.alt: Thermal infrared map of a city in false color — industrial areas and roads are hot, parks and water bodies are cool
image.caption: Urban heat island in false-color thermal imagery. As cities expand and green space shrinks, peak summer temperatures can exceed surrounding rural areas by several degrees. Satellite monitoring quantifies the effect precisely enough to guide urban greening policy.

body.heading: What it is all used for: real-world applications
paragraphs: Earth observation has long outgrown the scientific laboratory. It has become infrastructure underpinning modern agriculture, emergency management, climatology, archaeology, and financial analytics.

body.heading: Agriculture and forest cover
paragraphs: The vegetation index transformed crop assessment from an art into a science. United Nations food security agencies publish monthly yield forecasts for every region on Earth, drawing almost entirely on satellite data. In precision agriculture operations, farmers load weekly index maps into their tractors and apply fertilizer only where plants actually need it. Documented reductions of twenty to thirty percent in fertilizer use while maintaining or improving yield are real and repeatable outcomes across Europe and North America.

Amazon deforestation is tracked month by month through image comparison. Brazil's monitoring system detects new clearings within weeks of their onset — and there is strong evidence it works as a deterrent: during periods of active satellite monitoring, deforestation rates measurably declined. The satellite itself becomes a policy instrument.

body.heading: Disaster response
paragraphs: When a flood or earthquake strikes, the first need is understanding the scale of damage. Roads are cut off, communications are down. A radar image taken before and after the disaster allows responders to map flooded areas to individual city block resolution within hours. During the catastrophic Libyan floods of 2023 and the Pakistani floods of 2022, satellite data provided rescue teams with precise coordinates of cut-off communities before the first helicopter could reach those areas.

Wildfires are another domain where satellites are irreplaceable. Thermal sensors detect active fire fronts through dense smoke where aircraft cannot safely operate. Early warning systems in California and Australia now integrate real-time satellite feeds into fire spread prediction models.

body.heading: Climate monitoring
paragraphs: Arctic sea ice extent has been measured daily since 1979 — an unbroken record of nearly fifty years that is one of the primary indicators of climate change. Himalayan, Andean, and Greenland glaciers are measured via radar image differencing: not just surface area, but precise ice volume change including underwater erosion. Sea level is tracked by radar altimetry satellites accurate to a few millimeters per year.

The European Union's Copernicus program unified all of these data streams into a single open infrastructure. A constellation of six Sentinel satellite types in orbit provides free data to governments, researchers, businesses, and civil society worldwide. It is the largest open Earth observation program in history.

image.prompt: Photorealistic illustration for a science encyclopedia: false-color satellite view of Arctic Ocean showing dramatic sea ice extent reduction. Blue-white ice pack in upper portion giving way to dark open ocean. A dotted line overlaid indicates historical median ice edge from past decades for comparison. Hard sci-fi style, dark space border, data visualization overlay, monospace labels. Add the following text labels on the image: "current ice extent", "historical median edge", "open ocean", "multi-year ice".
image.alt: Satellite map of the Arctic showing reduced sea ice extent compared to the historical median from previous decades
image.caption: Daily satellite monitoring of sea ice has been continuous since the late 1970s. The reduction in Arctic sea ice extent is among the most comprehensively documented signals of ongoing climate change.

body.heading: Archaeology, urban planning, and intelligence
paragraphs: Lidar — a laser analog of radar — has revolutionized field archaeology. Laser pulses reflecting off terrain and vegetation allow analysts to "strip away" jungle canopy and see the structures hidden beneath: city walls, irrigation canals, roads belonging to long-vanished civilizations. That is how vast previously unknown urban complexes of Maya civilization in Guatemala and Belize were discovered during the 2010s.

Intelligence applications remain the least publicly discussed, but commercial satellites capable of thirty-centimeter resolution long ago made satellite reconnaissance accessible beyond state actors. During Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine beginning in 2022, imagery from Planet Labs and Maxar Technologies documented troop movements, urban destruction, and humanitarian crises in near-real-time — and those images were used by journalists, governments, and international courts as evidence in accountability proceedings.

body.heading: The tradeoff: resolution, revisit, and cost
paragraphs: There is no free lunch in Earth observation. High spatial resolution means a narrow imaging swath and infrequent revisit. Daily global coverage means accepting compromise on resolution or cost. This is not a technical limitation but a physical one: a larger telescope captures finer detail but sees a narrower field of view. To have both simultaneously requires tens or hundreds of satellites.

Three distinct market niches have therefore emerged. Large government programs — Landsat and Sentinel — provide open archival data at moderate resolution and weekly to bi-weekly revisit. Commercial very-high-resolution operators, led by Maxar with WorldView, sell thirty-centimeter imagery on demand at per-task pricing. Mass megaconstellations of small satellites, led by Planet Labs, fill the daily-revisit niche at a subscription price point.

The defining trend of the 2020s: launch costs fall as reusable rockets scale up, small satellites grow more capable, and artificial intelligence automatically processes petabytes of data that previously required hundreds of human analysts. The barrier to entry drops while capability rises. Over the coming decade, orbital Earth monitoring will likely become as pervasive and invisible as mobile internet — infrastructure so embedded in daily decisions that no one notices it anymore.

glossary.definition: Acquiring information about an object or phenomenon without physical contact. In the context of Earth, this means collecting data about the surface, atmosphere, and water bodies from satellites or aircraft.

An orbit at roughly seven hundred kilometers altitude with an inclination of approximately ninety-nine degrees to the equator. The orbital plane precesses to keep pace with Earth's revolution around the Sun, ensuring the satellite always crosses a given location at the same local solar time.
An index derived from the contrast between near-infrared and red reflectance. Measures the density and health of vegetation cover. Widely used in precision agriculture and forest monitoring.

An active microwave sensor that transmits its own signal and records the echo from the surface. Images independently of illumination and cloud cover. Used for ground subsidence detection, flood mapping, and sea ice monitoring.
Imaging in several discrete wavelength bands simultaneously — typically three to twelve channels. Allows surface types to be distinguished by their spectral characteristics.

A sensor recording hundreds of narrow contiguous spectral bands, producing a continuous spectral profile for every pixel. Enables identification of minerals, chemical compounds, and plant species by their unique spectral fingerprints.

The surface's own heat emission in the wavelength range of approximately eight to fourteen micrometers. Proportional to object temperature. Used for surface temperature mapping, wildfire detection, and urban heat island analysis.
The European Union's flagship Earth observation initiative. Operates a constellation of six Sentinel satellite families and provides free, open access to their data for any purpose.

A laser ranging instrument that measures distance to the surface by timing returning laser pulses. Used to build precise three-dimensional terrain models, detect buried archaeological structures, and monitor forest canopy height.

quiz.question: Why do Earth observation satellites use a sun-synchronous orbit?
options: To remain stationary above one region of Earth at all times
To pass over every point on Earth at the same local time of day, ensuring consistent illumination conditions for image comparison
To minimize fuel consumption for orbit maintenance
To avoid the radiation belts that would damage optical sensors
quiz.explanation: Sun-synchronous orbit ensures consistent solar illumination angle on every pass over the same location. Without this, images taken in different years would show different shadows and hues independent of any real surface change, making long-term comparison unreliable.

quiz.question: What is the primary advantage of synthetic aperture radar over a conventional optical camera?
options: Higher spatial resolution
Lower satellite manufacturing cost
Ability to image regardless of cloud cover, time of day, or illumination
Ability to capture true-color photographs
quiz.explanation: Synthetic aperture radar is an active sensor: it generates its own microwave signal. Microwaves pass through clouds, fog, and rain without significant attenuation, and no sunlight is required. This is critical in the tropics, polar regions, and anywhere rapid disaster monitoring is needed.

quiz.question: What does the normalized difference vegetation index measure and why is it useful?
options: Soil surface temperature
Water vapor concentration in the air column above a field
Vegetation density and health based on the contrast between near-infrared and red channel reflectance
Soil chemistry at one-meter depth
quiz.explanation: Healthy vegetation strongly absorbs red light for photosynthesis and simultaneously reflects near-infrared. The index captures that contrast: high values indicate healthy dense growth; bare soil and stressed or dry vegetation show low values. Agronomists use it to detect problems weeks before they become visible to the human eye.

quiz.question: Which program provides free, open-access Earth observation data from a constellation of six Sentinel satellite types?
options: NASA Landsat
Planet Labs
European Union Copernicus
ICEYE
quiz.explanation: The Copernicus programme is the European Union's flagship Earth observation initiative. It operates the Sentinel satellite family — six distinct mission types — and distributes their data freely and openly to anyone worldwide: governments, researchers, businesses, and the general public.

quiz.question: What distinguishes a hyperspectral sensor from a conventional multispectral satellite imager?
options: Hyperspectral sensors achieve higher spatial resolution
Hyperspectral sensors record hundreds of narrow contiguous spectral bands per pixel, enabling identification of minerals and chemical compounds
Hyperspectral sensors can image at night without illumination
Hyperspectral sensors transmit data to ground stations faster
quiz.explanation: A multispectral sensor captures a few broad spectral bands. A hyperspectral sensor records hundreds of narrow, adjacent bands, producing essentially a continuous spectrum for every pixel. Because different materials have unique spectral signatures, hyperspectral data can identify rock types, soil chemistry, and plant species without any field samples.
